title: Judgment on Samaria and Jerusalem
summary_text: Micah announces the LORD's coming judgment against Samaria and Jerusalem, and the mountains themselves are pictured as melting before him. The chapter names the idolatry and corruption that have spread through the land, then shows the prophet mourning over the coming ruin. It is a warning that covenant sin will not remain hidden.
key_points: The LORD comes forth from his holy place against Israel's sins.
Samaria is marked for ruin because of idolatry.
Micah weeps over the coming devastation.
The chapter links worship failure to public collapse.
evidence_phrases: Samaria as a heap of the field

title: Woe to the oppressors and promise to the remnant
summary_text: The prophet condemns those who plot violence and seize fields, houses, and inheritance from their neighbors. False prophets refuse to speak against them, but the LORD promises to gather a remnant and lead them out. The chapter moves from hard judgment to hope for the people who will follow God out of the breach.
key_points: The powerful steal land and homes from others.
False prophets reject correction.
The LORD promises to gather his people.
A breaker goes before them out of captivity.
evidence_phrases: Woe to those who devise iniquity
we will not prophesy to you
I will surely gather all of you
their king passes on before them

title: Corrupt rulers, prophets, and priests
summary_text: Micah turns directly against the rulers, prophets, and priests who misuse power for gain and then expect God to answer them in trouble. He says Zion will be plowed like a field because its leaders have perverted justice and sold their messages. The chapter is a severe indictment of official religion without righteousness.
key_points: Leaders eat people metaphorically by exploiting them.
Prophets speak for money instead of truth.
Priests teach for a price.
Zion will be destroyed as a result.
evidence_phrases: who hate the good and love the evil
her leaders judge for a bribe
Zion will be plowed as a field

title: Zion exalted among the nations
summary_text: The chapter looks ahead to a future when nations stream to the mountain of the LORD to learn his ways. War gives way to peace, and the scattered remnant is gathered and strengthened in Zion. Even as hostile nations assemble against her, the LORD's rule and purpose remain secure.
key_points: The mountain of the LORD becomes the center of learning.
Weapons are remade into tools of peace.
The LORD gathers and strengthens the remnant.
Zion is opposed but not abandoned.
evidence_phrases: they will beat their swords into plowshares
out of Zion will go forth the law

title: Bethlehem's ruler and the shepherd of peace
summary_text: Micah announces that a ruler will come from Bethlehem, one whose origins are from of old and whose rule will extend to the ends of the earth. He will shepherd his people in the strength of the LORD and bring peace by defeating Assyria and purging idols. The chapter ties humble origins to worldwide rule and secure shepherding.
key_points: The ruler comes from Bethlehem.
He shepherds his people in the LORD's strength.
Assyria cannot ultimately prevail.
Idols and false trusts are removed.
evidence_phrases: he will stand and will shepherd
this man will be our peace
I will cut off the cities

title: The LORD's case against his people
summary_text: The LORD brings a covenant lawsuit against Israel and reminds them of his saving acts from Balaam to the exodus. The people ask what offerings would please God, and the answer is not more sacrifice but justice, mercy, and humility. The chapter makes clear that true worship is measured by the life that follows it.
key_points: God recounts his saving deeds for Israel.
The people ask what sacrifice is enough.
The LORD answers with moral obedience.
Dishonest business practices are condemned.
evidence_phrases: What have I done to you?

title: Micah waits for God and hopes in mercy
summary_text: Micah laments the absence of faithful people and the collapse of trust in family and neighbor. Yet he chooses to wait for the LORD, who will bring him into the light and vindicate his cause. The book closes with confidence that God pardons iniquity and keeps covenant love.
key_points: Micah mourns the collapse of trust and loyalty.
He waits for the LORD instead of panicking.
God will bring light and vindication.
Mercy triumphs over guilt in the final hope.
evidence_phrases: I will look to the LORD
the LORD will be my light
Who is a God like you
